tldr: Mixpanel and PostHog share an event-based model, so the bulk of the swap is exporting raw events and people profiles, normalizing into PostHog's schema, and replaying through the /batch/ endpoint with historical_migration=true. The trickier work is concept mapping (alias/identify semantics, JQL → HogQL, group analytics) and rebuilding insights/funnels/dashboards on the PostHog side.
why_switch: Cost: Mixpanel bills on MTUs with an annual high-water-mark commitment that does not scale down within the contracted period; PostHog uses pure usage-based pricing with volume discounts and a free tier that already includes session replay and feature flags.
Open source / self-host: PostHog is OSS and offers self-hosted deployments for teams with strict data-residency or air-gap requirements — Mixpanel is closed SaaS only.
All-in-one: PostHog bundles product analytics, session replay, feature flags, A/B experiments, and surveys in one project — Mixpanel charges separately for replay and does not ship feature flags.
Raw SQL access: PostHog exposes HogQL (a SQL dialect over your event store) and a data warehouse for ad-hoc queries; Mixpanel's JQL is JavaScript-based and does not give you direct SQL.
Lower vendor lock-in: PostHog publishes its event schema and provides a documented batch capture API, so you can dual-write or move data out without proprietary connectors.
Autocapture: PostHog can autocapture pageviews, clicks, and form interactions out of the box, reducing instrumentation work — Mixpanel requires explicit track() calls for almost everything.
Active community: 238 HN mentions in the last 30 days vs 0 for Mixpanel signals stronger developer mindshare and a faster-moving roadmap.
what_to_export: Raw events with full property payloads (the source of truth — everything else can be rebuilt from this).
User / people profiles ($people / engage data) with custom properties.
Cohorts (definitions, not just static membership — re-derive in PostHog where possible).
Funnels (definitions: step events, conversion windows, breakdowns).
Insights / saved reports and their filter logic.
Custom JQL queries (you will need to translate these to HogQL).
Lookup tables / data dictionaries used for property enrichment.
Lexicon (event and property descriptions, schema annotations) — export as CSV if available.
Alerts and scheduled report subscriptions (rebuild manually; semantics differ).
Annotations / release markers used on charts.
export_method: Mixpanel exposes a Raw Event Export API (commonly referenced as /api/2.0/export on the data.mixpanel.com host, with data-eu.mixpanel.com for EU residency) that streams events as newline-delimited JSON. Authentication is via service-account credentials or project secret using HTTP Basic auth — verify the exact host, endpoint path, and auth scheme against your project's current Mixpanel docs before scripting, since Mixpanel has revised these over time. Profile / people data is pulled separately via the Engage API. Dashboards, funnels, and JQL queries do not have a single bulk export endpoint and typically need to be exported one by one through the UI or scripted via Mixpanel's Query API. For larger projects, Mixpanel's Data Pipelines / warehouse export to S3, GCS, Snowflake, or BigQuery is usually faster than streaming the export API and gives you a stable schema to transform from.
import_method: PostHog's batch capture endpoint is POST https://us.i.posthog.com/batch/ (or https://eu.i.posthog.com/batch/ for EU Cloud, or your self-hosted domain). The body is JSON with api_key, an events array under batch, and per-event event name + properties (which carry distinct_id). Per PostHog's docs, the request body must be under 20MB; there is no hard cap on event count per batch. For a backfill, set historical_migration: true on the request so events are flagged as a migration. People properties are sent via $set / $set_once on a $identify event using the same /batch/ endpoint. Feature flag definitions, dashboards, and insights are recreated through PostHog's REST API or via the UI — a 1:1 dashboard JSON import path is not guaranteed across tools, so plan to rebuild them. PostHog also offers a managed migration service for Mixpanel that automates export + transform + import, and a community-built tool (Stablecog's) is referenced in their docs — both are worth evaluating before writing custom code.

concept_mapping.target: PostHog distinct_id (same field name, but PostHog distinguishes anonymous $device_id vs identified user — formatting matters during migration)
PostHog $identify event (alias() exists in PostHog but its merge semantics differ; review carefully before mass-replaying)
PostHog Cohorts (static and dynamic both supported)
PostHog HogQL (SQL dialect, not JavaScript — full rewrite required)
PostHog Groups (concept exists in both, but group key registration and billing differ)
PostHog Data Management / taxonomy (event + property definitions)
PostHog Dashboards (rebuild; insight definitions don't translate 1:1)

effort: 2–6 weeks for a typical product team. ~3–5 days to set up the export pipeline and replay raw events through /batch/, ~1 week to remap distinct_id / identify semantics and validate person properties, ~1–2 weeks to rebuild dashboards, funnels, retention, and translate JQL → HogQL, plus 1–2 weeks of dual-running both systems to verify event counts and key metrics match before cutting over.
gotchas: distinct_id model differs: PostHog separates anonymous $device_id from the identified user_id and merges them on $identify. A naive replay can create duplicate persons if you don't preserve the original Mixpanel distinct_id and emit $identify events at the right points in the timeline.
Alias semantics aren't 1:1: Mixpanel's alias() and PostHog's alias() both link IDs but merge behavior and which ID 'wins' can differ — test on a sandbox project before bulk-importing.

Event naming conventions: Mixpanel commonly uses Title Case 'Page Viewed' style; PostHog conventions lean toward $pageview, $autocapture, snake_case custom events. Decide on a naming policy before import — renaming later breaks saved insights.

Autocapture overlap: if you turn on PostHog autocapture while also replaying historical Mixpanel pageview/click events, you'll double-count. Either disable autocapture for migrated event types or namespace migrated events.
JQL → HogQL is a full rewrite, not a translation: JQL is JavaScript map/reduce; HogQL is SQL. Budget time per saved query and don't expect a transpiler to do this for you.

Group analytics: both products support groups, but group property updates, billing impact, and group-level insights are modeled differently — re-validate any account-level / org-level reports after migration.
Deduplication on retries: if you re-run an export, set a stable per-event ID (PostHog's $insert_id or equivalent) on every replayed event; otherwise expect double-counting.
Historical events flag: always send historical_migration=true on the /batch/ payload during backfill so events are flagged correctly per PostHog's migration docs.

email.subject: Migrating from Mixpanel to PostHog — what to expect
email.body:
Hi,

If you're looking at moving product analytics off Mixpanel and onto PostHog, the short version is: the data model is similar enough that a migration is mostly a pipeline problem, not a re-instrumentation problem.

The core path is: pull raw events out of Mixpanel (the Raw Event Export API streams newline-delimited JSON; for larger projects the Data Pipelines warehouse export is faster), normalize to PostHog's schema, and replay through PostHog's batch capture endpoint at /batch/ with historical_migration=true. People profiles ride along via $identify with $set / $set_once. PostHog also has a managed migration service for Mixpanel and a community tool referenced in their docs — worth evaluating before writing custom code.

The gotchas worth flagging up front:

- distinct_id model: PostHog separates anonymous $device_id from identified user IDs and merges them on $identify. Replaying naively will create duplicate persons.
- JQL → HogQL is a rewrite, not a translation. JQL is JavaScript; HogQL is SQL. Budget per saved query.
- Autocapture overlap: if you turn on PostHog autocapture while replaying Mixpanel pageview/click events, you'll double-count.
- Dashboards, funnels, and retention definitions don't import cleanly — plan to rebuild them on the PostHog side.
- Always set historical_migration=true on the batch payload during backfill.

Realistic effort for a typical product team is 2–6 weeks, with the heaviest chunk being dashboard rebuilds and JQL → HogQL translation rather than the data move itself. I'd recommend dual-running both systems for at least a week before cutting Mixpanel off, so you can sanity-check event counts and key funnels.

Happy to dig into specifics — distinct_id strategy, group analytics, or the export pipeline — whichever is the bottleneck for you.

Best,
